prompt:
Create a therapeutic audio session for the following goal. Write the full script in English, approximately 15 minutes long when read aloud.

## Goal
Title: Process grief and keep the memory of grandpa alive
Description: Help Noah understand and express grief after the loss of his grandfather.

## Person
This is for Noah (age 7).
Developmental Tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD

## Research Evidence
The following research papers inform the therapeutic techniques to use:

1. "Child-centered grief therapy for bereaved children 6-10" (2023)
   Key findings: Memory boxes and continuing-bonds activities reduced grief symptoms and improved adjustment
   Therapeutic techniques: Memory box activity; continuing bonds narration; safe-place visualization

2. "Expressive arts in childhood bereavement" (2021)
   Key findings: Drawing and storytelling about the deceased improved emotional processing in 5-10 year olds
   Therapeutic techniques: Memory drawing; story narration; feelings thermometer; love never disappears reframe

## Instructions
- Create a complete, flowing therapeutic audio script
- Incorporate specific techniques and findings from the research above
- When clinical notes are available, weave their insights and observations into the session
- Personalize for Noah (age 7) (developmental tier: MIDDLE_CHILDHOOD)
- Target duration: 15 minutes when read aloud at a calm pace
- Write in English
- Follow the therapeutic audio content structure (warm introduction, understanding the challenge, guided practices, integration)
- Include a brief mention that a parent, caregiver, or professional can provide additional support if needed
- IMPORTANT: Do NOT use any markdown formatting (no **, ##, *, bullet points, or bold/italic syntax). Write plain spoken prose only, as the script will be read aloud by a text-to-speech engine
script:
Welcome Noah. I’m so glad you’re here with me today. I know you’ve been feeling sad because your grandpa isn’t here anymore. That’s a really big feeling, and it’s okay to feel that way. Today, we’re going to do some special things together to help your heart feel a little better, and to find ways to keep Grandpa’s memory alive and close to you. Are you ready? Let’s begin.

First, let’s get super comfy. You can sit or lie down, wherever you feel best. Maybe you have a favorite stuffed animal or a cozy blanket you want to hold. That’s a great idea. Now, let’s take a big, slow breath in through your nose… and let it out through your mouth like you’re blowing out birthday candles. Good. Let’s do that two more times. Breathe in… and blow out. Breathe in… and blow out. Good job.

You know, Noah, when someone we love very much, like your grandpa, isn’t here with us anymore, it can make our hearts feel all kinds of ways. Sometimes sad, sometimes confused, sometimes even a little angry. All of those feelings are okay. They are just your heart’s way of saying how much you love and miss him. And even though Grandpa isn’t here to give you a hug, the love you have for each other never, ever disappears. Love is a special thing that stays right here in your heart forever.

Now, I want you to imagine a very safe and happy place. It can be a real place, like your bedroom, or a pretend place, like a fluffy cloud or a secret fort. Picture it in your mind. What does it look like? Is it warm or cool? What colors do you see? This is your safe place. You can come here in your imagination anytime you feel a big feeling, like sadness. Just picture yourself there, feeling calm and safe. Let’s stay here for a moment… [pause]

Good. Now, from this safe place, I want you to think of a happy memory with your grandpa. It could be a time you played a game, or he read you a story, or maybe you just sat together. Picture it like a movie in your mind. What were you doing? What was Grandpa’s face like? Was he smiling or laughing? Try to see it all. This memory is a treasure, and you get to keep it forever.

We’re going to do something really special with that memory. In your mind, I want you to draw a picture of it. You don’t need real paper or crayons right now, just your imagination. In your mind, draw where you and Grandpa were. Draw what you were both doing. Maybe add some colors. What color was Grandpa’s shirt? What color was the sky or the room around you? Take your time and make this mental picture as bright and happy as you can. [pause]

That’s a beautiful memory picture, Noah. Now, let’s tell a short story about it. In your mind, or if you want, you can whisper it out loud. Start your story with “Once, Grandpa and I…” and then tell what happened in your memory. I’ll be quiet so you can tell the story to yourself. [longer pause]

That was a wonderful story. Thank you for sharing it, even if it was just with yourself. Holding onto stories like that is one of the very best ways to keep Grandpa’s memory alive. It’s like he’s right there in the story with you.

Now, let’s check in with your feelings. Imagine you have a feelings thermometer. At the bottom, it’s cool and blue for calm. At the top, it’s red and hot for big, strong feelings. Where is your feeling right now? Is it a little sad in the middle? Or maybe a little calm closer to the bottom? There’s no right or wrong answer. Just notice where it is. Sometimes just noticing our feeling can help it feel a little less confusing.

We’ve been talking about happy memories, and that’s so important. Another great way to remember Grandpa is to make something called a memory box. A memory box is a special box where you can keep little treasures that remind you of him. Maybe a photo, a seashell from a beach you visited together, a note, or a drawing you make of him. You can ask a grown-up who loves you, like your mom or dad, to help you find a box and start collecting these treasures. Anytime you miss him, you can open your memory box, hold the treasures, and remember your stories. Your love for him is in that box.

Let’s finish with one more calm breath. Breathe in slowly… and out slowly. Good.

You did such good work today, Noah. Remember, it’s okay to feel sad, and it’s also okay to feel happy when you think of Grandpa. You can visit your safe place in your mind anytime. You can tell your memory stories to your family, or draw them for real. And you can start your own memory box. These are all ways to carry Grandpa’s love with you always.

If your feelings ever feel too big or too heavy, that’s okay. You can always talk to a parent, a caregiver, or another grown-up you trust. They can give you a hug and help you with your memory box. You are brave, you are loved, and Grandpa’s love is always with you. Goodbye for now.
